justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — CA with AB5/minimum wage/pay transparency, Germany with works council approval, Indonesia with contractor classification and no tax withholding. (2) Germany works council approval identified as a hard legal pre-condition in BR-4 and escalation 3. (3) Driver classification data model difference identified in BR-6 and BR-7 (employee vs contractor affecting payroll fields). (4) Formal escalation for legal review in escalation 4 and escalation 3. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — AC-1 through AC-7 are jurisdiction-specific. (6) confidence.level = 52, well within the ≤60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on all three jurisdictions with specific legal citations (AB5, Labor Code §351, BetrVG §87)

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California (BR-1,2,3,8), Germany (BR-4,5), Indonesia (BR-6) with detailed legal citations. (2) Germany works council approval identified as a hard deployment gate/pre-condition (BR-4). (3) Driver classification data model difference explicitly identified (BR-7, employee vs contractor language, AC-5). (4) Formal escalations for legal review present — multiple structured escalations blocking California development until legal review and Germany until works council engagement. observations.text: Exceptional depth on California Labor Code §226 requirements — identified all nine mandated fields and specific penalty exposure ($50-$100 per driver per period).
Works council requirement treated as a hard deployment gate with feature toggle mechanism (works_council_approval_date must be non-null), not just a mention.
Driver classification data model difference addressed thoroughly — BR-7 explicitly forbids using 'Deduction' for contractors due to misclassification litigation risk.
Identified the subtle trap about employee vs contractor language as a misclassification evidence risk, going beyond just the data model to the legal implications.
Business impact analysis correctly identifies this as three distinct rollouts with different timelines, not a single global launch.
Edge cases are jurisdiction-aware — driver relocation, works council revocation, local minimum wage resolution, mid-cycle fee changes.
Multiple formal escalations with structured options and recommendations, including blocking California dev until legal review.
Confidence level of 52 appropriately reflects the legal complexity and underspecified nature of the input.
Indonesia correctly identified as voluntary/business feature with no legal obligation, using 'Service Fee' language to preserve contractor classification.
